subsection.title: Flow lines
subsection.p: A flow line of a vector field \mathbf F solves the differential equation \frac{d\mathbf r}{dt}=\mathbf F(\mathbf r(t)).
In the plane: x'=P(x,y), \qquad y'=Q(x,y).
In space: x'=P(x,y,z), \qquad y'=Q(x,y,z), \qquad z'=R(x,y,z).

subsection.title: Euler method
subsection.p: For \mathbf r'=\mathbf F(\mathbf r), Euler's method is \mathbf r_{n+1} = \mathbf r_n+\Delta t\,\mathbf F(\mathbf r_n).
It is simple but not very accurate.

program.input:
from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp
def ode(t, r): x, y = r return [Pfun(x, y), Qfun(x, y)]
sol = solve_ivp( ode, [t0, t1], [x0, y0], max_step=0.02 )
plt.plot(sol.y[0], sol.y[1]) plt.axis("equal") plt.show()

for r0 in initial_points: sol = solve_ivp(ode, [t0, t1], r0, max_step=0.02) plt.plot(sol.y[0], sol.y[1])
plt.axis("equal") plt.show()

subsection.title: Equilibrium points
subsection.p: Equilibrium points satisfy \mathbf F(\mathbf p)=\mathbf 0.
In the plane: P(x,y)=0, \qquad Q(x,y)=0.
Use symbolic solving:
Or numerical root finding if symbolic solving is too hard.

subsection.title: Linearization near an equilibrium
subsection.p: If \mathbf F(\mathbf p)=\mathbf 0, then near \mathbf p, \mathbf r' \approx J_{\mathbf F}(\mathbf p)(\mathbf r-\mathbf p).
The eigenvalues of J_{\mathbf F}(\mathbf p) help classify the local behavior.

subsection.title: Conservation law
subsection.p: A conservation law has the form \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot\mathbf J = s.
Here:
If there are no sources or sinks: \rho_t+\nabla\cdot\mathbf J=0.

subsection.title: Integral balance law
subsection.p: For a fixed region E, \frac{d}{dt}\iiint_E \rho\,dV = -\iint_{\partial E}\mathbf J\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS + \iiint_E s\,dV.
In words: \text{rate of stored amount} = -\text{outward flux} + \text{source production}.

subsection.title: Numerical balance check
subsection.p: At two nearby times, \frac{A(t+\Delta t)-A(t)}{\Delta t} approximates \frac{dA}{dt}.
Compare it to -\text{outward flux}+\text{source integral}.
This is a useful diagnostic for simulations.

subsection.title: Discrete divergence in two dimensions
subsection.p: On a grid with spacing \Delta x,\Delta y, for \mathbf J=(M,N), a central-difference approximation is \nabla\cdot\mathbf J \approx \frac{M(x+\Delta x,y)-M(x-\Delta x,y)}{2\Delta x} + \frac{N(x,y+\Delta y)-N(x,y-\Delta y)}{2\Delta y}.

subsection.title: Discrete divergence code

program.input: def divergence2(M, N, dx, dy): M_y, M_x = np.gradient(M, dy, dx) N_y, N_x = np.gradient(N, dy, dx) return M_x + N_y

subsection.p: Array-axis warning: \texttt{np.gradient} \text{ usually returns } y\text{-derivatives first.}

subsection.title: Advection by a velocity field
subsection.p: If density moves with velocity \mathbf v, then the flux density is \mathbf J=\rho\mathbf v.
With no sources: \rho_t+\nabla\cdot(\rho\mathbf v)=0.
If \nabla\cdot\mathbf v=0 and \rho is constant along moving particles, the flow is incompressible.

warning.p: Euler's method can be unstable for large time steps.
A good-looking picture may violate conservation.
Numerical diffusion can smear sharp features.
A vector field with a singularity should not be simulated through the singular point.
Always test a simulation on a case with a known answer.
